character_arcs.goal: to be part of the community in good standing
character_arcs.stakes: her membership in the only moral order she knows

story_constraints.prohibits: any villager speaking the lottery's lethal purpose aloud; naming it would rupture the ritual's performance
procedural objection to the selection process once underway — only outcome-protest is available

nodes.sketch: Warm summer morning. Villagers gather in the town square; children stack stones at the edge.
Mr. Summers arrives with the battered black box; heads of household are called by name in a ritual everyone knows but no one explains.
Bill Hutchinson draws the slip with the black dot; the family is isolated for a second round.
The Hutchinsons redraw. Tessie Hutchinson protests the procedure; she draws her own slip and finds the black dot.
Villagers close in. Stones are picked up. Tessie Hutchinson screams that it isn't fair. They stone her.

edges.realizes: the lottery is a standing annual institution with a designated official and a ritualized object
procedural authority — officiousness and cheerfulness — legitimizes random lethal selection
the family is the first selection stage; individual accountability follows group selection
individual selection activates undivided communal execution; Tessie's resistance changes nothing

edges.reframes: cheerful gathering becomes prelude to stoning
edges.withheld: from the reader, that the lottery's outcome is death

edges.reframes: the black box's battered officiousness becomes weapon-selection apparatus
edges.withheld: from the reader, what the box is for
